# My journey as a research assistant
In my first year of university at CSU Stanislaus, I learned about a program called ASPIRE STEM CRU, which paid students to do research for a professor at the university. I signed up and was reached out to by Dr. Ayat Hatem, a computer science professor focusing on data analysis and graph analysis. I joined her ongoing research on fake image detection, where we decided that it would be better to pivot to fake news detection due to the abundance of data samples and techniques available. 

We started by searching for various papers that describe the current methods used to determine if news are fake, where we came across various methods such as news content based, social context based, or hybrid. News content based methods focus on studying the text and looking for patterns suggesting false or misleading content such as clickbait words or mostly negative emotions/sentiment. Social context based look at the surrounding information about the post, such as the credibility of the author, unreliable sources, or posts being mainly shared by bots or fake accounts. Hybrid is a combination of the two, where you take into account both the signs of misinformation in the text as well as the surrounding engagement group to determine a final credibility score. Each have their pros and cons, and throughout recent decades people have been trying to determine a method to reliably determine whether a news article or social media post is misinformation before it's able to spread online. We gathered our findings and showcased them at ASPIRE's reverse career fair, where we showcased our findings to major employers and researchers in the area. Our poster submission is shown below.

<img src="/assets/images/posts/image_1782580554331.jpg" width="auto" alt="Poster showing fake news detection methods">

In our next semester, we decided to look more into social-context based methods and see what type of datasets we could find. We quickly found that there was only one major dataset, <a href="https://github.com/kaidmml/fakenewsnet" target="_blank">FakeNewsNet</a>, and this dataset had major problems that would prevent it from being usable, the biggest one being it's outdated. Because of this, we decided that we would create our own dataset of fake news posts and use it to perform an analysis on it later. We did research on various fact checking websites, and ultimately decided to use <a href="https://www.politifact.com" target="_blank">PolitiFact</a>, a fact checking site focused on political news and celebrities. My research partner, <a href="https://www.linkedin.com/in/alisson-ross/" target="_blank">Alisson Ross Salazar</a>, created a web scraper that could go through PolitiFact posts and extract information about the post as well as any X (Twitter) posts cited by them. These X posts were either posts that spread the false claim being proven wrong or posts that disagreed with the false posts and helped correct the misinformation. I took all of the X posts and created an X scraper using the data collected by Alisson. 

Using the X API, I took every link to x posts and scraped any relevant information from them. I ultimately created 3 interconnected master spreadsheets. One contained any relevant information about the post such as author, text, date, and rating from PolitiFact. The second contained the relationships between the post id and the retweeter id for every person that retweeted a given post. For example, if user 1 retweeted post 1, this csv file would contain a relationship between user 1 and post 1. If user 1 also retweeted post 5, their relationship would also be marked in the file, and we would be able to use these relationships to see connections between various posts. The final spreadsheet contained information about each of the retweeters of the posts, such as their usernames, follower count, account age, and if they were verified. These 3 interconnected spreadsheets would allow us to perform various tests on the network we captured and see any relationship between the different posts. The following graph shows the connection between the top retweeters and their post connection, showing how with just a quick filter we could quickly find a connection between the top retweeters of fake news.

**![](data:image/png;base64,)**
